southern_uk.location_description: The South Downs and Sussex coastal plain support a rich mix of chalk grassland, hedgerow, and woodland species — ideal conditions for pollinators and ground-nesting birds.

autumn.reason: Native to the Sussex chalk downs, field maple provides autumn colour and dense branching perfect for nesting birds in the South East.

nursery.name: Trees Direct
nursery.note: Native trees, bare root and pot grown

autumn.reason: Thrives on the alkaline soils of the South Downs, its vivid pink and orange berries are a critical autumn food source in this region.

nursery.name: Hedgerow Rose
nursery.note: Native hedgerow shrubs for southern England

autumn.reason: A chalk grassland native ideally suited to well-drained Sussex soils, it extends the foraging season for pollinators well into autumn.
autumn.wildlife: Common blue butterfly, Six-spot burnet moth, Buff-tailed bumblebee

winter.reason: A cornerstone of Sussex hedgerows, hazel catkins provide the first pollen of the year for queen bumblebees emerging in late winter.

nursery.name: Trees Direct
nursery.note: Native trees, bare root and pot grown

winter.reason: Widespread across the South East, holly berries are a critical winter food source when other fruits have gone.
winter.wildlife: Mistle thrush, Holly blue butterfly, Redwing

nursery.name: Hedgerow Rose
nursery.note: Native hedgerow shrubs for southern England

spring.reason: The native bluebell thrives beneath deciduous canopy across Sussex woodland edges, providing an early nectar source before most other plants flower.

nursery.name: British Wildflower Plants
nursery.note: Native bulbs and plugs, not Spanish hybrid

spring.reason: One of the first shrubs to flower in Sussex hedgerows, blackthorn blossom is invaluable for early emerging pollinators in March and April.
spring.wildlife: Brown hairstreak butterfly, Blackcap warbler, Hedgehog

nursery.name: Hedgerow Rose
nursery.note: Native hedgerow shrubs for southern England

spring.reason: A native of Sussex woodland margins and banks, primrose provides nectar when little else is available and is a larval plant for the Duke of Burgundy butterfly.
spring.wildlife: Duke of Burgundy butterfly, Early bee, Brimstone butterfly

summer.reason: A classic chalk grassland plant of the South Downs, it naturalises readily in lawns and borders and blooms prolifically through June and July.
summer.wildlife: Marbled white butterfly, Common carder bee, Meadow brown butterfly

summer.reason: Native to Sussex hedgerows and woodland edges, honeysuckle flowers attract hawk-moths on warm evenings and its berries feed warblers in late summer.
summer.wildlife: Hummingbird hawk-moth, Garden warbler, White admiral butterfly

summer.reason: Drought-tolerant and well-suited to the free-draining chalk soils of the South East, yarrow flowers from June to October and is highly attractive to hoverflies.
summer.wildlife: Thick-legged flower beetle, Common hoverfly, Painted lady butterfly

northern_uk.location_description: The Scottish Lowlands and upland fringes support acid grassland, moorland scrub, and riparian woodland — a landscape where native planting can directly connect with some of Europe's most ambitious rewilding projects.

autumn.reason: One of the most important native trees in Scottish upland landscapes, rowan berries are a critical autumn food source and the tree tolerates the cool, wet conditions of central Scotland well.
autumn.wildlife: Redwing, Fieldfare, Red grouse, Pine marten

nursery.name: Trees Direct
nursery.note: Native trees, bare root and pot grown

autumn.reason: Native to Scottish riparian edges and damp woodland, its heavy berry clusters persist well into winter providing food when other sources are depleted.

nursery.name: Celtic Wild Flowers
nursery.note: Native plants for Scotland and Wales

autumn.reason: The primary larval foodplant of the marsh fritillary butterfly, this is a key species for wet meadow habitats across central Scotland.
autumn.wildlife: Marsh fritillary butterfly, Common carder bee, Garden bumblebee

winter.reason: Better adapted to the wetter, cooler conditions of Scotland than silver birch, downy birch provides winter seed and bark habitat for specialist insects.

nursery.name: Trees Direct
nursery.note: Native trees, bare root and pot grown

winter.reason: The persistent hips of dog rose provide a vital winter food source in Scottish hedgerows when temperatures drop and other berries have gone.

spring.reason: An ancient woodland indicator species widespread across Scottish oakwood, wood anemone spreads slowly but reliably and signals genuine ecological restoration.
spring.wildlife: Buff-tailed bumblebee, Peacock butterfly, Wood mouse

spring.reason: Native to Scottish woodland edges, crab apple blossom provides abundant spring nectar and the fruit persists into deep winter — particularly valuable in upland areas.

nursery.name: Trees Direct
nursery.note: Native trees, bare root and pot grown

spring.reason: For wetter garden areas or pond margins, marsh marigold is a spectacular native Scottish wetland plant that flowers early when few other nectar sources exist.
spring.wildlife: Marsh fly, Early mining bee, Mallard

summer.reason: Native to Scottish roadsides and meadows, this robust perennial produces striking blue flowers from June to August and tolerates a range of soil types.
summer.wildlife: Common blue butterfly, Garden bumblebee, White-tailed bumblebee

summer.reason: A vigorous native climber found scrambling through Scottish hedgerows, tufted vetch fixes nitrogen and provides dense cover and nectar through midsummer.
A classic Scottish damp meadow plant, ragged robin thrives in wetter garden conditions and its distinctive pink flowers attract long-tongued bumblebees through July.
summer.wildlife: Garden bumblebee, Bee fly, Cuckoo bumblebee

european.location_description: Central European gardens sit at the intersection of Atlantic and Continental climate influences, supporting a diverse range of native woodland edge, meadow, and hedgerow species.

autumn.reason: Native across central Europe, wild cherry provides spectacular autumn colour, abundant blossom in spring, and cherries that support over 30 species of moth and butterfly larvae.
autumn.wildlife: Hawfinch, Common blackbird, Purple hairstreak butterfly

autumn.reason: One of the most wildlife-productive native shrubs in central Europe, elder berries in autumn are eaten by over 30 species of bird and its flowers support hundreds of insect species.

nursery.name: Chiltern Seeds
nursery.note: Native wildflower seeds, ships to Europe

autumn.reason: A native of calcareous grassland across central Europe, autumn gentian provides late-season nectar when most other flowers have finished.
autumn.wildlife: Clouded yellow butterfly, Small copper butterfly, Bumble bees

winter.reason: A long-lived native of European limestone country, yew berries are critical winter food for thrushes and waxwings, and its dense evergreen structure provides year-round shelter.

nursery.name: Trees Direct
nursery.note: Native trees, ships to most of Europe

winter.reason: Native to the limestone country of central Europe, wayfaring tree berries persist well into winter providing food for birds when other sources have gone.

spring.reason: A native of damp central European woodland, wild garlic carpets the ground in spring and provides nectar before the woodland canopy closes over.
spring.wildlife: Brimstone butterfly, Early bumblebee, Wood pigeon

spring.reason: The most wildlife-valuable native hedgerow shrub in Europe — its blossom supports over 150 insect species and its berries feed birds through autumn and winter.
spring.wildlife: Song thrush, Redwing, Brown hairstreak butterfly

spring.reason: One of the earliest native European spring flowers, providing a critical nectar source for queen bumblebees and early butterflies in March.
spring.wildlife: Early bumblebee, Small tortoiseshell butterfly, Pipistrelle bat

summer.reason: A native of dry European meadows, meadow clary produces tall spikes of vivid blue flowers through June and July, highly attractive to specialist bumblebee species.
summer.wildlife: Shrill carder bee, Great yellow bumblebee, Painted lady butterfly

summer.reason: The only native clematis in central Europe, traveller's joy provides dense summer cover for nesting birds and its fluffy seed heads persist through winter.
A native of dry European grassland, field scabious flowers from June to October providing a long season of nectar for specialist butterflies and bumblebees.
summer.wildlife: Marsh fritillary butterfly, Brown argus butterfly, Shrill carder bee

nursery.name: Chiltern Seeds
nursery.note: Native wildflower seeds, ships to Europe
